title: Your First Question: A Socratic Dialogue on a Familiar Topic
description: Practice philosophical inquiry by applying the Socratic method to a question you think you know the answer to. This session reveals how much remains uncertain when examined carefully.

steps.instruction: Choose a concept you are confident you understand: friendship, fairness, knowledge, beauty, or happiness. State your definition clearly in one sentence. Read about philosophical questioning -- what makes a question philosophical rather than factual?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have a clear initial definition to work with and understand what makes philosophical questions distinctive
steps.hint: Pick something familiar enough to define confidently -- that confidence will be productively challenged

steps.instruction: Apply the Socratic elenchus to your definition: find a counterexample that fits your definition but is not actually an instance of the concept, OR an instance of the concept that fails your definition. Read about the Socratic method.
steps.expectedOutcome: You have identified at least one case where your definition fails, revealing it was incomplete or too broad/narrow
steps.hint: For friendship: Is your acquaintance who does favors for you but doesn't care about you as a person a friend? Does your definition rule them out?

steps.instruction: Revise your definition to handle the counterexample. Now look for a new counterexample to the revised definition. Repeat 2-3 times. Read about logical fallacies -- are there any fallacies in your reasoning?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have gone through 2-3 revision cycles and have a more sophisticated definition -- or a better understanding of why the concept resists easy definition
steps.hint: If you are struggling to define it, you are doing philosophy correctly -- Socrates showed this was true of justice, virtue, and knowledge too

steps.instruction: Now consider: does your concept connect to ethics? Read about ethical frameworks -- is your concept (friendship, fairness, happiness) morally important? Why? Which ethical framework treats it as most central?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can trace connections between your concept and ethical theory, seeing how abstract concepts link to how we should live
steps.hint: Happiness is central to consequentialism (maximize it); friendship is important to virtue ethics (Aristotle devotes two books of the Ethics to it)

steps.instruction: Write a brief philosophical reflection: What did you learn? Did the Socratic process change your view? What is still uncertain? Is this kind of inquiry valuable -- and why?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have experienced philosophical inquiry from the inside and can reflect on its process and value
